abstract: Phenolic compounds, widespread secondary metabolites in plants, are known for their diverse biological activities and their propensity to interact with proteins, thereby modifying their structural and functional attributes. These interactions, occurring via both covalent linkages and non-covalent forces such as hydrophobic association, hydrogen bonding, and electrostatic interactions, have significant implications for human health and food science. This review examines the mechanisms underlying the formation of phenol-protein complexes and explores their physiological relevance and industrial utility. Depending on environmental conditions and the structural characteristics of the precursors—such as molecular weight and the presence of specific functional groups—these interactions can lead to protein folding or unfolding and the creation of either soluble or insoluble aggregates. Such changes directly influence the bioaccessibility, stability, and pharmacological potency of polyphenols, as well as the techno-functional properties of food proteins. A significant portion of this review is dedicated to the role of *in-silico* methodologies, particularly molecular docking and dynamics simulations, in elucidating these relationships. These computational tools provide a high-resolution window into site-specific binding and competitive conjugation, facilitating the virtual screening of phenolics against various protein targets. By integrating these theoretical insights with experimental data, researchers can better understand the transformative processes occurring during food processing and digestion. Ultimately, this knowledge is essential for engineering novel functional food ingredients, improving nutrient delivery systems, and developing potent nutraceuticals with optimized therapeutic profiles.
